summary.intent: Fix a browser session crash and ensure all sessions are logged to history, then debug a follow-up issue where Chrome failed to start in the sandbox container.
summary.outcome: The session crash fix (viewport resize on first screencast only) and session history logging were pushed. A subsequent Chrome startup failure was diagnosed as a dangling SingletonLock symlink and fixed by using symlink_metadata instead of exists() to detect and remove stale lock files.

repo: Repeated SetDeviceMetricsOverride calls destabilize Chrome in containers — viewport resize must only occur on the first screencast per session.
start_session uses INSERT OR IGNORE for idempotency so it can be called on every action without duplicating history entries.
The sandbox shared profile lives at sandbox/default/; old code used per-session directories, leaving no default/ directory on first run.
Chrome's SingletonLock is a symlink pointing to the container ID; when the container is gone the symlink is dangling.

code.finding: lock.exists() follows symlinks and returns false for dangling symlinks, so stale SingletonLock symlinks were never removed. Fix: use symlink_metadata() instead, and also remove SingletonCookie and SingletonSocket alongside SingletonLock.

workflow: On macOS, Path::exists() follows symlinks — use symlink_metadata() to detect dangling symlinks left by dead containers.
After fixing lock-cleanup logic, manually remove stale singleton files to unblock the running environment without requiring a restart.

friction: Chrome probed for 43+ seconds before the root cause (dangling SingletonLock symlink) was identified — no fast-fail or clear error from the container.
The exists() vs symlink_metadata() footgun on macOS is silent: the lock check appeared correct but silently skipped dangling symlinks.
Old per-session profile directories left over from the previous code meant the new shared default/ profile was never created, masking the real cause initially.
open_items: Old per-session profile directories in the sandbox remain and were not cleaned up — may want a migration or cleanup pass.
No fast-fail or timeout reduction when Chrome fails to start — 60s timeout with 1.4s probe interval means slow feedback on container failures.
Consider adding a container health check or startup log scraping so Chrome startup failures surface a clear error rather than silent probing.
